I don't know about the legality of HHO generators, but they are not allowed to be used in our country. It's still just a regulation and a ban. It's gotten to the point where even if you have a caravan on your property you have to have at least a notification to the building authority. Pretty soon you can't even breathe without a permit and taxation. :-(

line antifreeze ) . This makes a excellent little camping stove .
EDIT : 
You must cut two " v" notches I the tomato can at the bottom of the can . ( so the fuel can wick  ) .
You can put a pan etc on top of this stove . It's quite capable of boiling
two cups of water w/less than one
oz. of fuel .

author: @Chris153758
text: Glycerol is essentially the propane molecule (C3H8) with 3 of the H replaced with hydroxyl (-OH) groups, aka alcohol groups. So it is similar to carbohydrates, like sugars. It would be more surprising if it wasn't flammable. It's surprising how much different the physical characteristics of propane and glycerol are, just by substituting 3 H for 3 -OH groups.

author: @davidjones8680
text:
Back in the early 1970s I left school and started work at an electroplating company.  They used glycerine in the plating processes and at the on set of the winter the transport manager would take a 5 gallon tub of glycerine and tip it into the big diesel storage tank before it was replenished. When i asked why he did this he said it was to stop the diesel fuel from waxing up in very cold weather, and it also got rid of any water particals in the diesel storage tank. Back then there was no such thing as winter diesel with adatives to stop it waxing up. He apparently learnt this when he was in the British Army driving tank transporters.  In 1977 I purchased my first diesel car, and the first time it got to minus weather i would put a pint of "G" in the tank before filling it up, I never ever had any waxing issues.
David in Dudley.

On ships we use heavy fuel that needs to be about 100C or around 200 F also.
We have to endure all the pipes are hot and pipe steam inside copper lines inside the insulation to keep it warm when shut down.

Yes I know about starting on diesel letting things warm up switching to heavy fuel to run and then switching back to light fuel to flush the lines by running for some time .

I am not sure how you will use glycerine in a heater practically. Heat it it on a stove 
Then pour in the fuel tank?
What happens to the left over in the tank when it gets cold?

Anyway I am Very happy to learn this from this video just wanted to warn people it is not a simple switch but I encourage use !

Maybe you can dilute the Glycerin so that you get more total gallons of fuel?

author: @Dr-Sy
text:
If an electric current creates a magnetic field (then a magnetic field creates an electrical field), So could you take a ring magnet place an electric cable from the mains through the centre of a ring magnet (found in a microwave). Then take another magnet and wrap some copper wire around that magnet (creating a coil). Then when you bring the 2 magnets close together, The electric current from the electrical wire that goes through magnet one increases the magnetic field, Magnet two with the coil around it would pickup the magnetic field of magnet one.  (Magnet two would never touch magnet one only needs to be near the magnetic field and would generate electricity). 

Therefore, Electric wire creating a magnetic field, going through a ring magnet, which will make the magnetic field stronger, then bring in another magnet that has a coil and when it gets close enough to the first magnet the magnet wire the coil would increase its magnetic field therefore creating more electricity.

I hope you get what I mean.

author: @johnbrizendine7716
text: The only bad thing about glycerin is that it will absorb and mix with the waxes in diesel fuel and turn into a thick sludge, so you have to be very careful when mixing it with other fuels.  I have seen many fuel tanks sludged up from improperly "washed" bio diesel blends.  If you do mix glycerin with regular fuels, make certain that you add some iso-alcohol and diesel fuel detergents/fuel system cleaners to it as well to keep it from turning into a thick sludge and clogging up the fuel system.  I had a 1987 mercedes 300d with a 3 liter inline 6 turbo diesel that ran heated veg oil, hydraulic oil, motor oil, (all used and filtered)  It was really nice, it got about 40MPG and was really fast.  Unfortunately, old woman ran a red light and hit me and totalled it.  I REALLY miss that car!!!!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: you don't want to mix it with diesel - but that sounds unlikely - you would probably have to mix in an emulsifier - after all it naturally seperates in the biodiesel production method

replies.author: @oriweinberg-z3v
replies.text: how can i use crude glycerin 80% into diesel generators and fire Camins ?

replies.author: @johnbrizendine7716
replies.text: @oriweinberg-z3v If you mix it with iso alcohol or methanol (NOT ETHANOL) and just a bit of diesel fuel, you shouldn't have too much trouble with it, but be careful glycerin is VERY hygroscopic, so it will soak up more water than ethanol gasoline (E85) and will likely trash your fuel system if you just leave it in there for more than a few weeks at a time.  Glycerin burns a bit better than diesel, but turns to sludge so you have to really watch it closely.

author: @Marian_Martin_Morgenstern
text:
Since when is Glycerine deemed "Non-flammable"? Have you never seen what happens if you mix Potassium Permanganate with Glycerine?
That mix is self-igniting! Hell Yeah! And it burns like  there is no tomorrow too. And some survivalists even use it as a fast (and dangerous) Firestarter
Just don't add sulphuric acid to the Permanganate beforehand, at least if you want to keep your Hands… and Eyebrows… and Face and, well everything else of your body intact and sticking together.
That is definitely a thing only for the professional Chemists to do. Like NileRed, NurdRage or Thoisoi2.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that's what the research papers say mate

replies.author: @Marian_Martin_Morgenstern
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering 
Its no diethyl-ether, I get it, but that one has fumes that burst into flames if you look at it to hot!
On the other Hand, as a trained chemist, I tend to look at safety data sheets more then research papers... seeing that its what you look at if you have to handle chemicals on a daily basis and those say 1,2,3-propanetriol is a combustible.
Its an Alcohol, why shouldn't it burn?

replies.author: @Marian_Martin_Morgenstern
replies.text:
​ @Robert Murray-Smith  Alas, I found where this misconception might stem from!
OSHA!
A quote from a safety data sheet if found online 
"Special Hazards Arising from the Substance: This product is not a flammable liquid per the OSHA Hazard communication standard, but will ignite and burn at temperatures exceeding the flash point.
Spontaneous combustion may occur under high temperature, closed conditions if the material is absorbed in various fiber matrices and oxygen is present (i.e. oily rags). For additional fire related
information, see NFPA 30."
Someone in the US of A does think that it isn't flammable if they say it isn't…

author: @mrab4222
text: At 0:35 you said it's "inflammable". "Inflammable" ("inflammable" from "inflame") does not mean "non-flammable", it means the same as "flammable". (I think there was a difference in that "flammable" meant it could burn whereas "inflammable" meant that it could burst into fire.) The "in-" means "into", not "non-". The word "flammable" was introduced because it's less confusing.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate

author: @aaronfranklin324
text:
It's funny. I just woke up. Am smoking some minty vape syrup from a glass tube with a lighter. ( Much cleaner and less dangerous than electricvapes with 600 degrees wire embedded in silicone foam). 
Well mostly safer.
Fairly often the glycerol explodes I'm not careful.
Or as Murray just said as it happened,
It boils out the end and drips flaming napalm on me.🌋🏴󠁧󠁢󠁷󠁬󠁳󠁿

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that's an interesting way of vaping - I am going to look into that! funny how something never occurs to you until someone mentions it - cheers mate

replies.author: @aaronfranklin324
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering you get pretty fed up with electric ones, what with batteries lasting a week when you can't get 12V chargers less than 2amps now. 
For the small ones.
And the electronics and charging connectors last a few months at best in geothermal areas, or marine environments.
I had a funny experience in a Rotorua vape shop when I got sold one with a pre broken tank. And they wouldn't replace or refund  2 hours later.
Pulled out a mini cigar tin and a lighter, told the idiot and everyone in the shop, that we might as well go back to the elegant old fashioned way.
The manager accused me of bringing  "illegal Paraphernalia" and tresspassed me from all the their nationwide chain of stores. 
So I went straight to the central police station, asked for the senior Sargent on duty. And convinced him to pose for a picture with me vaping from my cigar tin in front of the station. 🤭🤗
It helps to burn off the plastic and burnish a cup shaped depression in the tin if you're going to do this.
Funnily I find it's very difficult to convince pub staff that you aren't breaking any laws or rules doing this in their smoking areas. 🙄

replies.author: @josephpadula2283
replies.text:
I bought the first edition of Tickell’s book.
Many  of errors in it explaining how Diesel engines worked.
I was teaching at A&M at the time in Marine Engineering Technology in Texas.


I contacted him provided corrections and sent him some copywriter free USNavy drawings.
He used them in the second edition.

All this is because my son in California mentioned biodiesel
And I said I have the best book on DIY by a guy I talked to years ago and sent some photos too.
Joshua Tickell.
Turns out he lives in my son’s town 
And he waited in him several times while doing a gap year.

I think he no longer supports his own earlier work as diesels are Bad now.

If you think this is a big change , environment activists in CA are now Pro nuclear power!

You can’t make this up.

replies.author: @RichardKAlbion
replies.text:
@josephpadula2283 That's an interesting story, thank you for that. I lost my original edition after lending it to a friend who never returned it. I bought another copy recently. I wonder if it has the drawings in that you sent him? I shall have to have a look at it and see.
It is interesting how nuclear has gone from dirty to green recently. I remember in the early 80's a whole food shop near where I lived which was run by some hippies had a huge painting on the side of their building saying "Nuclear power? No thanks" I wonder what they think these days? It used to make me chuckle watching them locking up on an evening and getting in their beat up old VW van and driving off as it belched a huge clouds of smoke behind it.

author: @crankfwd8209
text:
Is there a biofuel that can be used in jet engines?
And is that glycerine still burning on your work top?

replies.author: @C-M-E
replies.text:
Jet engines are actually about the most versatile combustion engine on the planet for fuel choice. Basically anything that burns and can be pumped will work. Propane, LPG, diesel, kerosene, gasoline, alcohol, hydrogen... 
About the only thing that changes between them is the energy density which will affect how much power you can get out of them. The caloric value if you will. Biodiesel is fairly nice to use when you can get it in huge quantity as it self-lubricates to a degree, and as you may have guessed, because jet engines absolutely devour fuel.

replies.author: @crankfwd8209
replies.text: @C-M-E thanks, at least there is some hope for the planet. Do you know if there is a clean version of rocket fuel?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yes carbon material engineering gives a great answer to your question and yes it burnt from when I lit it until after the vid

replies.author: @michaelbevan3285
replies.text: @C-M-E that's why gas turbines in in such use for power generation. I worked on industrial units that would run on natural gas from the sea, as a byproduct of oil extraction. There were also units running on regular diesel and heated lighter weight Marine grades in the same industrial site and there were even diesels that ran on natural gas. A lot of the units were converted aero engines, such as RR Avons, Orendas, Pratt and Whitney ST-8s and so on.

text: one important fact: vapes are not composed of poly ethylene glycol, that is highly toxic, and is used as engine coolant. author: @teenagemutantninjaraver2224
text:
Be very careful burning glycerin.

The glycerine by-product burns well, but unless it's properly combusted at high temperatures it will release toxic acrolein fumes, which mainly form at between 200 and 300 deg C (392-572 deg F)

replies.author: @HeatAndPower
replies.text: ^^^THIS 😦

replies.author: @HeatAndPower
replies.text:
Yeah vaping glycerin and burning glycerin are NOT the same thing… you don’t get acrolein from vaping glycerin.


This is HOW acrolein is produced commercially, by burning glycerin.

replies.author: @testurenergy
replies.text: Yeah acrolein is very toxic.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: just check out the paper I mentioned for a gas analysis

replies.author: @dnomyarnostaw
replies.text: @HeatAndPower "Researchers have recently found that vegetable glycerin can lead to inflammation of the lungs due to long-term vaping or e-cigarette use. One study on the dangers of vaping glycerin ran for four weeks, during which 34 were randomized to using flavor- and nicotine-free e-cigs or to a control group of no-use. At the end of the study, researchers discovered that the side effects of inhaling vegetable glycerin included inflammation in the lungs. The study also found that those who smoked e-cigarettes twice a day for a month contained higher levels of chemicals like propylene glycol in their blood"

author: @pixelpatter01
text: Whatever you had in that beaker was not pure glycerine. The flashpoint of glycerine is 160 °C, so there is NO way the liquid you had on the rag was giving emitting enough vapor to ignite if it was glycerine. Glycerine has a few problems you haven't mentioned as a fuel; it can polymerize to form tars and Acrolein which is a toxic gas.

replies.author: @justinw1765
replies.text: It is mixed with ethanol or methanol as per the last video, most likely.

replies.author: @testurenergy
replies.text:
^THIS. Acrolein is very toxic and if glycerin isn’t burned hot enough acrolein will form. 

This is literally how commercial acrolein production is done, by burning glycerin.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: no it isn't - it's the remains of the biodiesel I made - but then I am just demonstrating for full details you need to read the paper I pointed out

author: @christiankrippenstapel4336
text:
Caution! Glycerol ist neither miscible with dino-diesel nor biodiesel. Surely you can make dispersions, but that leads to massive clogging of normal paper-based fuel filters, so glas-fibre filters are more suitable.
If you want to use glycerol as fuel, spare yourself the transesterification, install a preheating device in your car and drive with neat plantoil. Or buy an old Diesel-Benz with prechamber technology - these cars run with furniture polish either 😉

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: there are lots of things you can burn - it's not a question of that really mate

replies.author: @cherylm2C6671
replies.text: I'm taking an alt-fuel class and was going to mix up a batch using lemon oil. What's that about a diesel pre-chamber? My fingers are itching to experiment and 3d printing is baked science. I wonder how long that glycerine burned...

replies.author: @christiankrippenstapel4336
replies.text:
@cherylm2C6671 "Pre-chamber" diesel means an older technology where diesel fuel is injected and burned in the so called "pre-chamber" and not in a cavitation of the piston like in modern direct-injector engines.
The drawback of pre-chamber diesel engines is the higher fuel consumption, but on the other hand they are very robust against dirty and off-spec fuel like plant-oil.

replies.author: @peetsnort
replies.text: Does the old VW golf have prechamber.? I used to put straight canola and sunflower oil and it ran perfectly

replies.author: @christiankrippenstapel4336
replies.text: @peetsnort Most of th older diesels before 1990 had prechamber engines and are suitable für plantoil after conversion with pre-heating devices or two tanks.

replies.author: @darrylgordon5170
replies.text: He used the glycerine that he produced when he made the bio diesel. So, yes. It would contain the excess methanol and lye that was not used in the reaction.  Both of which could be recovered to reduce cost and purify the glycerine.  In the video, it looked to me that the initial flame had an alcohol flame. That could be why it took a flame so readily.  However, as he just stated, it doesn't matter. Heating the glycerine lowers the viscosity to the point where it could be used directly in a diesel engine.  This is the same process utilized to run straight vegetable oil in most diesel engines. I say "most" because there are some that will eat it without having to heat it.  The older Ford 7.3 liter comes to mind.

author: @jimurrata6785
text: Are you going to do butanol as a replacement for petrol next?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I don't think so

replies.author: @jimurrata6785
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering That's a shame as it's been proven as a direct replacement for gasoline and there's simple pathways using bioengineered bacteria to produce this tetra carbon alcohol from glycerol.
It doesn't seem to have any of the ill effects of, say, ethanol and is very close to petrol in energy.
